evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go centralizes protocol-wide (`ProtocolActionSpecs`) and upload-specific (`UploadActionSpecs`) action metadata in one file, then exports flattened `AllActionSpecs`, which creates a hub boundary for multiple domains.

goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go and goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go both implement cross-package parity checks against `contracts.UploadActionBindingSpecs()`/`contracts.ActionContractBindings()`, indicating coupling is being enforced by duplicated test orchestration across modules rather than one shared verification seam.

No import-time execution or mutable package-level initialization side effects were found in the assigned files; coupling risk here is structural registry concentration and duplicated cross-module validation logic.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: Action contract governance is still concentrated in one catalog hub and verified through duplicated cross-module test choreography, increasing blast radius when contract shapes evolve.

evidence: Large function-pointer runtime bags are used as primary dependency carriers: importerRuntime embeds importerStorageRuntime/importerUploadRuntime/importerProvisionRuntime with many callbacks (goseg/importer/importer.go:102-139), StartupRuntime and startBackgroundServicesRuntime compose many callback groups (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:205-269), and orchestration Runtime*Ops types form another broad callback matrix (goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:17-137).

Multiple call paths add wrapper layers with minimal behavior change: StartBroadcastLoop -> StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime -> runBroadcastLoop/runBroadcastTickWithRuntime (goseg/broadcast/loop.go:114-221), startBackgroundServices -> startBackgroundServicesWithRuntime -> buildBackgroundServices -> resolveStartBackgroundServicesRuntime (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:1003-1041,527-537), and AreSubdomainsAliases -> AreSubdomainsAliasesWithRuntime -> areSubdomainsAliases (goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:391-397,368-389).

Runtime construction/merge/validate scaffolding repeats across modules (resolve+defaults+validate pattern): WiFi runtime service (goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go:21-90), startup runtimes (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:519-559), importer runtime validation families (goseg/importer/importer.go:189-219), increasing indirection overhead for ordinary call flows.

RuntimeConfig keeps LinuxUpdates as an anonymous inline struct (goseg/structs/configs.go:189-192), and callers must repeat that shape via inline literals when applying defaults/patches (goseg/defaults/defaults.go:198-204, goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:720-726), which leaks data-shape details across modules.

Test seams rely heavily on mutable package globals (`SetRuntime` pattern across click modules; singleton setters in broadcast/events/auth), which encourages serial test orchestration and raises cross-test contamination risk.

Long-running/background behavior uses goroutines and real-time constructs in production paths (`runAuthCleanupLoop` ticker in `goseg/auth/lifecycle/lifecycle.go`, loop controller + ticker in `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`) with limited explicit lifecycle ownership at call sites.

Command execution paths in `goseg/docker/lifecycle/commands.go` and `goseg/accesspoint/router.go` include hardcoded polling/sleep behavior that is harder to validate deterministically in integration-style tests.

Orchestration runtime composition is split across many function-pointer bags and forwarding methods (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go`), creating a broad seam surface with repetitive wiring logic.

Config update flow duplicates domain shape and patch semantics across multiple hand-maintained layers (`goseg/structs/configs.go`, `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`), increasing structural drift risk.

Several large test files combine many unrelated behavior families into single files/functions, which reduces local cohesion and makes fixture setup dominate intent (`goseg/main_test.go`, `goseg/handler/systemsvc/system_test.go`, `goseg/importer/importer_test.go`).
goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go calls startram service creation in reconciliation (lines 144-145) without checking returned errors, while nearby persistence paths aggregate and return errors.

goseg/broadcast/loop.go records failures by storing err.Error() into state via AddSystemTransitionError (line 212), and goseg/broadcast/state.go stores only strings, dropping typed error identity and cause chains.

Long-running loops use inconsistent escalation policies: goseg/broadcast/loop.go keeps running after every tick failure, but goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go escalates after thresholded consecutive failures.

findings.summary: Contract boundary enforcement is split across a central hub plus duplicated cross-module test choreography.
evidence: `AllActionSpecs()` in `specs.go` aggregates multiple domain families (protocol + upload) into one exported registry surface.
`fixtures_test.go` builds independent fixture/binding maps and validates canonical action-contract alignment.
`actions_test.go` separately re-derives expected upload binding behavior and payload rule checks from the same contracts source.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single contract-conformance harness in `protocol/contracts` that exposes reusable assertions/fixtures for action binding invariants, and split catalog definitions by bounded context (e.g., protocol-core vs upload) with composition at package boundary entrypoints; consumers/tests should call shared conformance helpers instead of rebuilding parity logic.

findings.summary: Oversized callback-bag runtimes hide true dependencies and reduce abstraction leverage.
evidence: importerRuntime embeds three runtime structs and carries many unrelated callbacks in one value (goseg/importer/importer.go:102-139).

StartupRuntime and startBackgroundServicesRuntime aggregate large callback sets for unrelated concerns (init, transitions, logging, startram) (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:205-269).
orchestration runtime splits into many Runtime*Ops bags that are still assembled centrally by buildRuntimeSeamBundle (goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:17-137,179-191).
main defines additional runtime containers (bootstrapRuntime/c2cRuntime and nested runtime structs), continuing the same pattern (goseg/main.go:58-203).
findings.suggestion: Replace wide callback bags with narrow per-use-case interfaces (for example: uploader storage, upload authorization, provisioning, background-service launcher) and inject only the interface needed by each workflow; keep one composition root that wires concrete implementations.

findings.summary: Repeated public->WithRuntime->internal wrapper chains add call depth with little policy value.
evidence: Broadcast lifecycle uses multiple forwarding wrappers where each layer mostly validates nil/defaults then forwards (goseg/broadcast/state_lifecycle.go:13-23,48-57; goseg/broadcast/loop.go:114-121,195-206).

Startup background services chain through startBackgroundServices, startBackgroundServicesWithRuntime, buildBackgroundServices, and runtime resolver before real work begins (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:1003-1041,527-537).
Alias checks expose three entrypoints where two are direct forwards to areSubdomainsAliases (goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:391-397,399-408).
findings.suggestion: Standardize each flow to a single exported entrypoint plus one internal core function; keep default-runtime creation at the edge and remove extra forwarding aliases that only pass through unchanged arguments.

findings.summary: Runtime resolve/merge/validate scaffolding is duplicated across modules instead of shared patterns.
evidence: WiFi service re-resolves runtime in constructor and prepareForUse, then validates callback completeness again (goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go:28-39,67-77).
WiFi runtime has separate default+merge constructor semantics (goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go:157-180).
Startup and importer each implement their own resolve-with-defaults + grouped validation systems (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:519-559; goseg/importer/importer.go:189-219).
findings.suggestion: Introduce one shared runtime-seam helper pattern (or package-level generic helper) for merge+validate+resolve lifecycle, then apply it consistently so modules define dependencies once and avoid repeated scaffolding code paths.

findings.summary: Anonymous nested config structs force cross-file shape duplication and brittle patching.
evidence: RuntimeConfig.LinuxUpdates is declared as an anonymous inline struct rather than a named domain type (goseg/structs/configs.go:189-192).
Defaults must restate the same inline struct literal to seed values (goseg/defaults/defaults.go:198-204).
Config patch application also redefines that inline shape when assigning updates (goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:720-726).
findings.suggestion: Promote LinuxUpdates to a named struct type in `structs` and reference it in RuntimeConfig, defaults, and patch application so callers reuse one stable type instead of repeating anonymous shape literals.

findings.summary: Conformance tests omit non-action contract families, leaving governance gaps.
evidence: Conformance tests assert action fixture coverage and upload payload-rule consistency but contain no checks that StarTram contract specs are present/valid.
README describes a contract conformance gate including family-governance, but this batch’s conformance tests only enforce action-family behavior.
findings.suggestion: Add family-level conformance tests that enumerate all catalog families (including StarTram) and fail on missing/extra contract specs, then wire those tests into the same contract gate path documented in README.

findings.summary: Owner metadata contracts drift between catalog modules.
evidence: Action catalog defines owner values behind `OwnerModule` typed constants and casts to string at emission time.
StarTram catalog publishes owner metadata as raw string constants with no shared typed owner contract.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared owner type/registry in the contracts layer and require all catalog modules to emit owner metadata through that shared type to prevent silent divergence.

findings.summary: Contract policy authority is split across docs, comments, and tests.
evidence: README defines required backend contract gate behavior and scope.
uploadsvc package docs define payload-rule governance location, while runtime conformance is separately encoded in tests across two packages.
No single executable policy source is referenced by both the contract catalog and all governance tests.
findings.suggestion: Define one machine-readable governance entrypoint in `protocol/contracts` (family registry + required invariants), generate or drive conformance tests from it, and reduce README/doc comments to pointers to that executable source.

findings.summary: Runtime orchestration uses a highly fragmented seam model with repeated forwarding and default wiring that obscures module boundaries.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines many independent `*Ops` structs with function fields and multiple default constructors (`defaultRuntimeContainerOps`, `defaultRuntimeUrbit`, `defaultRuntimeStartupOps`, etc.), each wiring cross-package dependencies.

`runtime_core.go` mirrors this with repeated nil-check forwarding methods (`LoadWireguard`, `LoadMC`, `LoadMinIOs`, `LoadNetdata`, `LoadUrbits`, `LoadLlama`) and merge/option boilerplate for each ops group.
Tests then need broad callback setup to satisfy this shape, indicating orchestration construction concerns are spread instead of encapsulated.
findings.suggestion: Collapse function-field bags into a smaller set of cohesive domain interfaces (e.g., startup loader, container lifecycle, startram integration) and centralize nil/fallback policy in a single adapter layer so call sites consume higher-level capabilities rather than dozens of callback slots.

findings.summary: Configuration shape and patch behavior are duplicated across schema and struct layers, creating a systemic maintenance hotspot.
evidence: `structs/configs.go` defines canonical config sections (`RuntimeConfig`, `ConnectivityConfig`, etc.) while `config_update_schema.go` separately defines parallel patch structs and field registries.
`config_update_schema.go` uses a manually assembled registry (`init` + `buildConfigPatchByKey` + parse/apply/merge hooks) with per-field parsers, increasing touch points for each config key.

Large merge/config tests in `config_test.go` reflect this complexity with many field-by-field assertions, suggesting high structural coupling between representation, patch parsing, and merge semantics.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single schema source-of-truth for config fields (code-generated or declarative metadata) that drives patch parsing, apply, and merge wiring, so new fields are added once and propagated mechanically.

findings.summary: Transition event broker APIs are hand-duplicated per event type and per wrapper, producing repetitive structure with low leverage.
evidence: `events.go` repeats publish/get methods for four transition types in both `EventRuntime` and `DelegatingEventBroker`, with near-identical nil-check/delegate logic.
This explicit duplication propagates type-specific boilerplate into callers that consume transition buses, reinforcing pattern repetition rather than a normalized broker primitive.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a typed transition bus abstraction (or small generated layer) that centralizes publish/subscribe + delegate-null handling once, then expose thin typed accessors only where domain clarity needs it.

findings.summary: Multiple tests mix broad scenario coverage and heavy runtime wiring in single files/functions, reducing test design coherence.
evidence: `main_test.go` defines `withC2CState` with many related function parameters and uses large inline runtime assembly for varied behaviors.
`system_test.go` contains broad multi-action tests (e.g., swap + wifi + update paths together) with large dependency structs configured inline.

`importer_test.go` includes substantial helper/runtime mutation logic at top-level, and `startup_orchestrator_test.go` similarly builds layered runtime option helpers, indicating repeated harness construction patterns.
findings.suggestion: Split large behavior-aggregate tests into focused action-specific suites and extract shared fixture builders into per-package test harness modules with scenario presets, so each test configures only the seams it actually exercises.

findings.summary: Startram reconciliation silently ignores service-creation failures and can continue with inconsistent state.
evidence: In reconcilePatpState, startram.SvcCreate(patp, "urbit") and startram.SvcCreate("s3."+patp, "minio") are invoked without error checks (rectify_services.go:144-145).

The same file otherwise treats reconciliation/persistence failures as first-class errors using errors.Join and returned errors (e.g., lines 103-123, 173-188), so these unchecked calls are inconsistent with the local strategy.
Existing tests in rectify_test.go cover transition application but do not assert behavior when service-creation fails, leaving this inconsistency unguarded.
findings.suggestion: Capture both SvcCreate errors, aggregate with errors.Join, and propagate through reconcilePatpState/Reconcile; add tests that inject SvcCreate failures and assert returned error plus non-success transition behavior.

findings.summary: Broadcast state stores only error strings, losing typed causes and preventing consistent downstream handling.
evidence: runBroadcastTickWithRuntime appends failures with AddSystemTransitionError(err.Error()) (broadcast/loop.go:212), flattening wrapped/sentinel errors to plain text.
AddSystemTransitionError in broadcast/state.go stores []string only (lines 66-77), so callers cannot use errors.Is/errors.As or preserve causal chains.
Tests assert string equality against gotErr.Error() (broadcast/state_test.go:202-204, 248-249), codifying string-only semantics rather than structured error propagation.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a structured transition error payload (code, message, cause class) and preserve original error identity internally; adapt AddSystemTransitionError and tests to validate structured fields instead of raw error strings.

findings.summary: Background workers apply incompatible retry/escalation rules, creating unpredictable failure behavior across subsystems.
evidence: broadcast.runBroadcastLoop keeps looping indefinitely on repeated tick errors and only invokes tickErrorFn (broadcast/loop.go:184-188) without any built-in fail threshold.
dockerSubscriptionHandlerWithRuntime tracks consecutive failures and returns fatal after a configured limit (subsystem_events.go:235-261).

Startup supervision in startup_orchestrator.go relies on worker errors for health decisions (e.g., waitForFailFastErrors), so divergent per-loop policies produce inconsistent startup/runtime failure observability.
findings.suggestion: Standardize a shared background-worker error policy (budget, backoff, escalation) and apply it to broadcast and docker loops so repeated failures are handled consistently and surfaced predictably to startup health checks.

findings.summary: Cross-module workflows are coupled through mutable package-level singletons, creating hidden dependency direction and broad blast radius.
evidence: Broadcast loop and transitions default to `DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime()` rather than requiring explicit runtime boundary.
Event publication in chop service depends on `events.DefaultEventRuntime()` singleton.
Click modules expose package-global runtime instances (`runtimeRuntime`, `runtimeBackup`, `runtimeRestore`, `runtimePack`) and mutable `SetRuntime` hooks.
Auth accessors repeatedly pull global manager through `GetClientManager()` instead of explicit dependencies.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit subsystem-level dependency containers passed from composition root; keep package globals only as compatibility adapters. Gradually migrate call sites to constructor-injected runtimes and make singleton use opt-in at boundaries.

findings.summary: Session/client-manager nil handling is inconsistent, producing mixed panic-vs-error behavior across auth paths.
evidence: `runAuthCleanupLoop` directly calls `session.GetClientManager().CleanupStaleSessions(...)` with no nil guard.
`GetMuConn`, `WsIsAuthenticated`, `WsAuthCheck`, and `WsNilSession` dereference `GetClientManager()` directly; `TokenIdAuthed` in same file does guard nil.
`sessionStore.AddToAuthMap` and `RemoveFromAuthMap` call `store.getClientManager()` methods without validating the pointer.
findings.suggestion: Define one canonical manager-access contract (e.g., `requireClientManager() (*ClientManager, error)`), use it in all auth/authsession call paths, and ensure goroutine loops degrade safely when manager is unavailable.

findings.summary: Click subpackages use repeated single-implementation runtime interfaces and pass-through wrappers that add indirection with little behavioral leverage.
evidence: `clickRuntime` methods mostly delegate one-to-one to function fields without adding policy/translation.
Backup/restore/pack each repeat the same `interface + defaultRuntime + global var + SetRuntime/reset/get` pattern.
No alternate runtime implementations are present in production code for most interfaces, indicating abstraction overhead outpacing practical polymorphism.
findings.suggestion: Replace repeated per-package runtime interfaces with a smaller shared command-executor abstraction and focused option structs; keep only seams that enforce real policy or isolation.

findings.summary: Testing seams are mostly mutable globals, which weakens isolation and makes concurrent/parallel test execution fragile.
evidence: Modules expose mutable runtime singletons via package variables and setters, requiring test-order discipline to avoid leaked state.
Global default runtime setters in events/broadcast alter process-wide behavior and can interfere across tests running in parallel packages.
This seam style favors unit substitution but does not provide clear scenario-level integration contracts.
findings.suggestion: Adopt constructor-based fixtures for subsystem runtimes and use explicit test harness objects per test case; reserve global overrides for legacy entrypoints only and gate them behind helper APIs that auto-restore state.

findings.summary: Several core operational files combine orchestration, IO, validation, and state mutation responsibilities, reducing local coherence.
evidence: Accesspoint router file handles shell-safety validation, startup lifecycle, firewall rules, and process control in one unit.
Broadcast loop file combines controller lifecycle, tick scheduling, state assembly, transition preservation, and publish logic.
Click runtime file mixes hoon file lifecycle, container exec transport, response parsing, and multiple near-duplicate command APIs.
findings.suggestion: Split each file into role-focused components (command builder/validator, transport executor, orchestration coordinator, state assembler) with narrow interfaces and shared error contracts.
